However, this was put on hold so she could concentrate on recording Destiny Fulfilled, the final studio album by Destiny's Child. The album was released in November 2004 and marked a significant moment in the group's history, as it would be their last project together before an indefinite hiatus. Destiny's Child embarked on a farewell tour to support the album, allowing fans around the world to celebrate the group's iconic legacy and contributions to music. Meanwhile, Beyoncé began laying the groundwork for her solo career, preparing to establish herself as a formidable force in the industry independent of the group that had launched her to fame.

Beyoncé's second solo album B'Day was released on September 5, 2006, in the US, to coincide with her twenty-fifth birthday. It sold 541,000 copies in its first week and debuted atop the Billboard 200, becoming Beyoncé's second consecutive number-one album in the United States. The album's lead single "Déjà Vu", featuring Jay Z, reached the top five on the Billboard Hot 100 chart. The album showcased a more dance-oriented and edgy sound compared to her debut, with production from Timbaland and The Neptunes that gave B'Day a contemporary edge. Critical reception was generally positive, with reviewers praising Beyoncé's vocal versatility and the album's infectious production, though some felt it lacked the cohesive narrative of her previous work.

Her first acting role of 2006 was in the comedy film The Pink Panther starring opposite Steve Martin, grossing $158.8 million at the box office worldwide. Her second film Dreamgirls, the film version of the 1981 Broadway musical loosely based on The Supremes, received acclaim from critics and grossed $154 million internationally. In it, she starred opposite Jennifer Hudson, Jamie Foxx, and Eddie Murphy playing a pop singer based on Diana Ross. The film earned her a Golden Globe nomination and solidified her status as a leading actress in Hollywood. Her performance was praised for its emotional depth and vocal prowess, showcasing her ability to carry a major musical drama. By the end of 2006, she had established herself as one of the year's most bankable and critically respected stars.

On April 4, 2008, Beyoncé married Jay Z. She publicly revealed their marriage in a video montage at the listening party for her third studio album, I Am... Sasha Fierce, in Manhattan's Sony Club on October 22, 2008. I Am... Sasha Fierce was released on November 18, 2008 in the United States. The album formally introduces Beyoncé's alter ego Sasha Fierce, conceived during the making of her 2003 single "Crazy in Love", selling 482,000 copies in its first week, debuting atop the Billboard 200, and giving Beyoncé her third consecutive number-one album in the US. The album featured the number-one song "Single Ladies (Put a Ring on It)" and the top-five songs "If I Were a Boy" and "Halo". Achieving the accomplishment of becoming her longest-running Hot 100 single in her career, "Halo"'s success in the US helped Beyoncé attain more top-ten singles on the list than any other woman during the 2000s. It also included the successful "Sweet Dreams", and singles "Diva", "Ego", "Broken-Hearted Girl" and "Video Phone". The album's commercial success extended internationally, with I Am... Sasha Fierce becoming a global phenomenon and solidifying Beyoncé's status as one of the most commercially successful artists of her generation. Critics praised the album's ambitious production and diverse sonic palette, which blended pop, R&B, and dance influences across its twenty-seven tracks. The project demonstrated Beyoncé's artistic growth and willingness to experiment with different personas and musical styles, earning multiple Grammy Award nominations. In support of the album, Beyoncé embarked on the I Am... Tour, which became one of the highest-grossing concert tours by a female artist at that time. The success of I Am... Sasha Fierce established a new peak in her career, setting the stage for continued dominance in the music industry throughout the following decade.

Beyoncé further expanded her acting career, starring as blues singer Etta James in the 2008 musical biopic, Cadillac Records. Her performance in the film received praise from critics, and she garnered several nominations for her portrayal of James, including a Satellite Award nomination for Best Supporting Actress, and a NAACP Image Award nomination for Outstanding Supporting Actress. Beyoncé donated her entire salary from the film to Phoenix House, an organization of rehabilitation centers for heroin addicts around the country. On January 20, 2009, Beyoncé performed James' "At Last" at the First Couple's first inaugural ball. The iconic performance became a memorable moment in presidential history, watched by millions of viewers who tuned in to witness the historic inauguration. Following this success, Beyoncé continued to balance her music career with selective acting roles, choosing projects that aligned with her artistic vision and social values. Her work in Cadillac Records demonstrated her ability to embody complex historical figures and deliver nuanced dramatic performances beyond her established reputation as a recording artist. This period solidified her status as a multifaceted entertainer capable of commanding respect across both the music and film industries.

At the 52nd Annual Grammy Awards, Beyoncé received ten nominations, including Album of the Year for I Am... Sasha Fierce, Record of the Year for "Halo", and Song of the Year for "Single Ladies (Put a Ring on It)", among others. She tied with Lauryn Hill for most Grammy nominations in a single year by a female artist. In 2010, Beyoncé was featured on Lady Gaga's single "Telephone" and its music video. The collaboration became an instant hit, showcasing both artists' distinctive styles and generating millions of views on YouTube. That same year, Beyoncé also embarked on her highly successful "I Am... World Tour," which became one of the highest-grossing concert tours by a female artist at that time.

Beyoncé announced a hiatus from her music career in January 2010, heeding her mother's advice, "to live life, to be inspired by things again". During the break she and her father parted ways as business partners. The singer used this time away from the spotlight to focus on personal growth and spent time traveling and exploring new creative inspirations that would eventually influence her later work. She also concentrated on her marriage to Jay-Z, whom she had married in 2008, and the couple began planning for their future family together.

In 2011, documents obtained by WikiLeaks revealed that Beyoncé was one of many entertainers who performed for the family of Libyan ruler Muammar Gaddafi. Rolling Stone reported that the music industry was urging them to return the money they earned for the concerts; a spokesperson for Beyoncé later confirmed to The Huffington Post that she donated the money to the Clinton Bush Haiti Fund. Following the donation, Beyoncé faced mixed reactions from critics who questioned whether the gesture fully addressed the ethical concerns surrounding her initial performance for a regime with a documented history of human rights abuses.

Her fourth studio album 4 was released on June 28, 2011 in the US. 4 sold 310,000 copies in its first week and debuted atop the Billboard 200 chart, giving Beyoncé her fourth consecutive number-one album in the US. The album was preceded by two of its singles "Run the World (Girls)" and "Best Thing I Never Had", which both attained moderate success. The fourth single "Love on Top" was a commercial success in the US. The album showcased a more experimental and electronic sound compared to her previous work, with production from notable collaborators including Kanye West, Timbaland, and The Dream. Critics praised Beyoncé's versatility and vocal performance throughout the record, though some felt the album lacked a cohesive narrative. 4 would go on to become one of the best-selling albums of the decade, cementing Beyoncé's status as one of the most commercially successful artists of her generation.

On January 7, 2012, Beyoncé gave birth to her first child, a daughter, Blue Ivy Carter, at Lenox Hill Hospital in New York. The arrival of her daughter marked a significant milestone in Beyoncé's life, as she balanced her role as a new mother with her continued dominance in the music industry.

In January 2013, Destiny's Child released Love Songs, a compilation album of the romance-themed songs from their previous albums and a newly recorded track, "Nuclear". Beyoncé performed the American national anthem singing along with a pre-recorded track at President Obama's second inauguration in Washington, D.C. The following month, Beyoncé performed at the Super Bowl XLVII halftime show, held at the Mercedes-Benz Superdome in New Orleans. The performance stands as the second most tweeted about moment in history at 268,000 tweets per minute. At the 55th Annual Grammy Awards, Beyoncé won for Best Traditional R&B Performance for "Love on Top". Following her successful performances, Beyoncé began working on her fifth studio album, which would mark a significant departure from her previous work. The album, titled "Beyoncé," was released on December 13, 2013, as a surprise visual album with no prior announcement or traditional marketing campaign. This unconventional release strategy generated massive buzz and broke multiple streaming records within hours of its debut. The self-titled album showcased a more experimental sound and featured collaborations with artists such as Jay-Z, Drake, and Frank Ocean, solidifying Beyoncé's position as one of the most innovative artists of her generation.

Beyoncé embarked on The Mrs. Carter Show World Tour on April 15 in Belgrade, Serbia; the tour included 132 dates that ran through to March 2014. It became the most successful tour of her career and one of the most-successful tours of all time. In May, Beyoncé's cover of Amy Winehouse's "Back to Black" with André 3000 on The Great Gatsby soundtrack was released. The track received widespread critical acclaim for its sultry reinterpretation of the classic soul song, showcasing Beyoncé's versatility as an artist. Following the success of both the tour and soundtrack contribution, she continued to solidify her status as one of the most influential entertainers of her generation.

On December 13, 2013, Beyoncé unexpectedly released her eponymous fifth studio album on the iTunes Store without any prior announcement or promotion. The album debuted atop the Billboard 200 chart, giving Beyoncé her fifth consecutive number-one album in the US. This made her the first woman in the chart's history to have her first five studio albums debut at number one. Beyoncé received critical acclaim and commercial success, selling one million digital copies worldwide in six days; The New York Times noted the album's unconventional, unexpected release as significant. Musically an electro-R&B album, it concerns darker themes previously unexplored in her work, such as "bulimia, postnatal depression [and] the fears and insecurities of marriage and motherhood". The single "Drunk in Love", featuring Jay Z, peaked at number two on the Billboard Hot 100 chart. The album's visual component, featuring an accompanying visual album with music videos for each track, revolutionized how artists could present their work in the streaming era. Critics praised the production work of artists like The-Dream, Timbaland, and Ryan Tedder, who crafted sonically adventurous soundscapes that complemented Beyoncé's vocal performances. The album's introspective nature marked a significant departure from the celebratory tone of her previous work, offering fans a glimpse into her private struggles and vulnerabilities. Other notable tracks from the album, including "Pretty Hurts" and "Formation," became cultural touchstones that sparked conversations about identity, self-image, and social issues. The surprise release strategy subsequently influenced how major artists approached album launches, with many attempting their own unannounced drops in the years following. Beyoncé's fifth album ultimately solidified her status as one of music's most innovative and influential artists of her generation.

At the 57th Annual Grammy Awards in February 2015, Beyoncé was nominated for six awards, ultimately winning three: Best R&B Performance and Best R&B Song for "Drunk in Love", and Best Surround Sound Album for Beyoncé. She was nominated for Album of the Year but the award was won by Beck for his Morning Phase album. In August, the cover of the September issue of Vogue magazine was unveiled online, Beyoncé as the cover star, becoming the first African-American artist and third African-American woman in general to cover the September issue. She headlined the 2015 Made in America festival in early September and also the Global Citizen Festival later that month. The performances showcased her artistic evolution and cemented her status as one of the most influential entertainers of the era. Throughout the remainder of 2015, Beyoncé continued to expand her creative endeavors, collaborating with other artists and working on new material that would eventually define the next chapter of her career. Her dominance across music, fashion, and cultural conversations made it clear that her impact extended far beyond the recording industry itself.
On February 6, 2016, one day before her performance at the Super Bowl, Beyoncé released a new single exclusively on music streaming service Tidal called "Formation".

Beyoncé is believed to have first started a relationship with Jay Z after a collaboration on "'03 Bonnie & Clyde", which appeared on his seventh album The Blueprint 2: The Gift & The Curse (2002). Beyoncé appeared as Jay Z's girlfriend in the music video for the song, which would further fuel speculation of their relationship. On April 4, 2008, Beyoncé and Jay Z were married without publicity. As of April 2014, the couple have sold a combined 300 million records together. The two music superstars kept their marriage largely private for the first few years, rarely making public appearances together or discussing their relationship in interviews. However, they began to collaborate more openly on music, with Jay Z featuring on Beyoncé's hit single "Drunk in Love" in 2013, which became a massive commercial and critical success. Their partnership extended beyond music into business ventures, as they became one of the most influential power couples in the entertainment industry. Together, they have continued to break records and dominate charts while maintaining a relatively guarded personal life away from the spotlight.

In August, the couple attended the 2011 MTV Video Music Awards, at which Beyoncé performed "Love on Top" and started the performance saying "Tonight I want you to stand up on your feet, I want you to feel the love that's growing inside of me". At the end of the performance, she dropped her microphone, unbuttoned her blazer and rubbed her stomach, confirming her pregnancy she had alluded to earlier in the evening. The announcement sent shockwaves through the crowd and instantly became one of the most memorable moments in VMAs history, with the gesture sparking countless headlines and celebratory reactions from fans around the world.

On January 7, 2012, Beyoncé gave birth to a daughter, Blue Ivy Carter, at Lenox Hill Hospital in New York under heavy security. Two days later, Jay Z released "Glory", a song dedicated to their child, on his website Lifeandtimes.com. The song detailed the couple's pregnancy struggles, including a miscarriage Beyoncé suffered before becoming pregnant with Blue Ivy. The emotional track resonated deeply with fans and critics alike, who praised Jay Z's vulnerability and the couple's willingness to share such a personal journey. " Glory" became a defining moment in their relationship, showcasing how they transformed their pain into art that connected with millions of people worldwide.

Beyoncé and husband Jay Z are friends with President Barack Obama and First Lady Michelle Obama. She performed "America the Beautiful" at the 2009 presidential inauguration, as well as "At Last" during the first inaugural dance at the Neighborhood Ball two days later. Beyoncé and Jay Z held a fundraiser at the latter's 40/40 Club in Manhattan for Obama's 2012 presidential campaign which raised $4 million. Beyoncé uploaded pictures of her paper ballot on Tumblr, confirming she had voted in support for the Democratic Party and to encourage others to do so. The couple's political engagement extended beyond financial contributions, as they became vocal advocates for various social justice causes throughout the 2010s. Beyoncé's visual album "Lemonade" in 2016 featured imagery and themes that resonated with Black Lives Matter activists, while Jay Z's Roc Nation label began investing in criminal justice reform initiatives. Their friendship with the Obamas remained strong even after leaving the White House, with the former president and first lady attending several of Beyoncé's major performances and events over the years.

In an interview published by Vogue in April 2013, Beyoncé was asked if she considers herself a feminist, to which she said, "that word can be very extreme... But I guess I am a modern-day feminist. I do believe in equality". She elaborated that feminism, for her, meant having the freedom to make choices—whether that meant pursuing a career, starting a family, or both—without judgment or limitation. Beyoncé emphasized that she wanted her daughter Blue Ivy to grow up understanding that women deserved the same opportunities and respect as men in every aspect of life.

In 2015 Beyoncé signed an open letter which the ONE Campaign had been collecting signatures for; the letter was addressed to Angela Merkel and Nkosazana Dlamini-Zuma, urging them to focus on women as they serve as the head of the G7 in Germany and the AU in South Africa respectively, which will start to set the priorities in development funding before a main UN summit in September 2015 that will establish new development goals for the generation.

Following the death of Freddie Gray, Beyoncé and Jay-Z, among other notable figures, met with his family. They offered their condolences and discussed ways to support the Gray family during their time of grief, while also pledging their commitment to advocacy work around issues of police accountability and racial justice.

Forbes magazine began reporting on Beyoncé's earnings in 2008, calculating that the $80 million earned between June 2007 to June 2008, for her music, tour, films and clothing line made her the world's best-paid music personality at the time, above Madonna and Celine Dion. They placed her fourth on the Celebrity 100 list in 2009 and ninth on the "Most Powerful Women in the World" list in 2010. The following year, Forbes placed her eighth on the "Best-Paid Celebrities Under 30" list, having earned $35 million in the past year for her clothing line and endorsement deals. In 2012, Forbes placed Beyoncé at number 16 on the Celebrity 100 list, twelve places lower than three years ago yet still having earned $40 million in the past year for her album 4, clothing line and endorsement deals. In the same year, Beyoncé and Jay Z placed at number one on the "World's Highest-Paid Celebrity Couples", for collectively earning $78 million. The couple made it into the previous year's Guinness World Records as the "highest-earning power couple" for collectively earning $122 million in 2009. For the years 2009 to 2011, Beyoncé earned an average of $70 million per year, and earned $40 million in 2012. Her financial success continued to grow exponentially throughout the following decade, with her 2013 self-titled visual album marking a significant turning point in her career trajectory and commercial dominance. The surprise release strategy and innovative multimedia approach generated unprecedented streaming numbers and album sales, further solidifying her position as one of the entertainment industry's most bankable stars. By 2014, Beyoncé's net worth had climbed substantially, bolstered by her Formation World Tour, which became one of the highest-grossing tours by a female artist in history. Forbes consistently ranked her among the top-earning celebrities globally, recognizing not only her musical talent but also her shrewd business acumen and ability to diversify her income streams. Her partnership with Jay Z extended beyond their personal relationship into professional ventures, including the music streaming service Tidal, which demonstrated their ambition to reshape the digital music landscape. As she entered her fourth decade, Beyoncé's influence transcended mere financial metrics, establishing her as a cultural icon whose economic impact reflected broader shifts in how artists could maintain control over their work and maximize their earning potential.

Beyoncé's vocal range spans four octaves. Jody Rosen highlights her tone and timbre as particularly distinctive, describing her voice as "one of the most compelling instruments in popular music". While another critic says she is a "Vocal acrobat, being able to sing long and complex melismas and vocal runs effortlessly, and in key. Her vocal abilities mean she is identified as the centerpiece of Destiny's Child. Her mastery of various vocal techniques allows her to seamlessly transition between powerful belting and delicate, nuanced phrasing. Critics and fans alike have noted her ability to adapt her voice across multiple genres, from R&B and hip-hop to soul and pop, while maintaining her signature sound. This versatility has made her one of the most influential vocalists of her generation, inspiring countless artists to push the boundaries of their own vocal capabilities. Her technical proficiency, combined with her emotional depth and artistic interpretation, solidifies her legacy as one of the greatest singers in music history.

Beyoncé's music is generally R&B, but she also incorporates pop, soul and funk into her songs. 4 demonstrated Beyoncé's exploration of 90s-style R&B, as well as further use of soul and hip hop than compared to previous releases. The album showcased her versatility as an artist and solidified her status as a trendsetter in the music industry. Critics praised her willingness to experiment with different sounds while maintaining the sophisticated vocal delivery and production quality that her fans had come to expect.

She has received co-writing credits for most of the songs recorded with Destiny's Child and her solo efforts. Her early songs were personally driven and female-empowerment themed compositions like "Independent Women" and "Survivor", but after the start of her relationship with Jay Z she transitioned to more man-tending anthems such as "Cater 2 U". This shift reflected her evolving perspectives on love and partnership, showing a more vulnerable and domestic side of her artistry. Over time, she would continue to balance both themes, crafting a diverse catalog that spoke to different chapters of her personal and professional journey.

In 2001, she became the first African-American woman and second woman songwriter to win the Pop Songwriter of the Year award at the American Society of Composers, Authors, and Publishers Pop Music Awards. Beyoncé was the third woman to have writing credits on three number one songs ("Irreplaceable", "Grillz" and "Check on It") in the same year, after Carole King in 1971 and Mariah Carey in 1991. She is tied with American songwriter Diane Warren at third with nine songwriting credits on number-one singles. ( Her contributions to popular music have been recognized across multiple genres, from R&B to hip-hop to pop, establishing her as one of the most versatile and influential songwriters of her generation. These achievements solidified her position not just as a performer, but as a creative force behind some of the biggest hits of the 2000s. Her songwriting prowess would continue to evolve throughout her career, earning her numerous Grammy Awards and further cementing her legacy in music history.

Beyoncé names Michael Jackson as her major musical influence. Aged five, Beyoncé attended her first ever concert where Jackson performed and she claims to have realised her purpose. When she presented him with a tribute award at the World Music Awards in 2006, Beyoncé said, "if it wasn't for Michael Jackson, I would never ever have performed." She has frequently cited his innovative approach to choreography, music videos, and overall artistry as instrumental in shaping her own career trajectory and creative vision. Beyoncé has also spoken about how Jackson's ability to transcend racial and cultural boundaries inspired her to pursue music on a global scale. Throughout her career, she has paid homage to his legacy through her own groundbreaking performances and visual presentations, continuing the tradition of artistic excellence that he established.

The feminism and female empowerment themes on Beyoncé's second solo album B'Day were inspired by her role in Dreamgirls and by singer Josephine Baker. Beyoncé paid homage to Baker by performing "Déjà Vu" at the 2006 Fashion Rocks concert wearing Baker's trademark mini-hula skirt embellished with fake bananas. The iconic costume choice was both a celebration of Baker's legacy as a groundbreaking Black entertainer and a visual statement about reclaiming and reimagining historical symbols of Black female artistry. Through this performance, Beyoncé demonstrated how contemporary artists could draw inspiration from their predecessors while creating new meanings and contexts for their work.

Beyoncé has stated that she is personally inspired by US First Lady Michelle Obama, saying "She proves you can do it all" and she has described Oprah Winfrey as "the definition of inspiration and a strong woman". She has also discussed how Jay Z is a continuing inspiration to her, both with what she describes as his lyrical genius and in the obstacles he has overcome in his life. Beyoncé has expressed admiration for the artist Jean-Michel Basquiat, posting in a letter "what I find in the work of Jean-Michel Basquiat, I search for in every day in music... he is lyrical and raw". Beyond these personal influences, Beyoncé has credited her mother, Tina Knowles, as her foundational inspiration, crediting her with instilling confidence and work ethic from an early age. She has also spoken about how other artists like Prince and David Bowie shaped her artistic vision, particularly in their fearless approach to reinvention and genre-blending. These varied inspirations—spanning from political figures and entertainment icons to visual artists and family members—have collectively informed the multifaceted creative approach that defines her career across music, film, and visual art.

In 2006, Beyoncé introduced her all-female tour band Suga Mama (also the name of a song in B'Day) which includes bassists, drummers, guitarists, horn players, keyboardists and percussionists. Her background singers, The Mamas, consist of Montina Cooper-Donnell, Crystal Collins and Tiffany Moniqué Riddick. The ensemble became known for their high-energy performances and tight musicianship, elevating the production value of Beyoncé's live shows. This groundbreaking all-female band set a new standard for concert tours and has influenced countless artists to prioritize diverse representation in their backing musicians.

Beyoncé has received praise for her stage presence and voice during live performances. Jarett Wieselman of the New York Post placed her at number one on her list of the Five Best Singer/Dancers. According to Barbara Ellen of The Guardian Beyoncé is the most in-charge female artist she's seen onstage, while Alice Jones of The Independent wrote she "takes her role as entertainer so seriously she's almost too good." Her commitment to perfecting every aspect of her performances has earned her comparisons to legendary entertainers like Michael Jackson and Prince. Critics consistently note that Beyoncé's meticulous attention to choreography, vocal delivery, and audience engagement sets a standard that few contemporary artists can match.

Described as being "sexy, seductive and provocative" when performing on stage, Beyoncé has said that she originally created the alter ego "Sasha Fierce" to keep that stage persona separate from who she really is. She described Sasha as being "too aggressive, too strong, too sassy [and] too sexy", stating, "I'm not like her in real life at all." Sasha was conceived during the making of "Crazy in Love", and Beyoncé introduced her with the release of her 2008 album I Am... Sasha Fierce. The alter ego allowed Beyoncé to explore more daring artistic directions while maintaining a clear boundary between her public performance identity and her private self. Though she eventually retired the Sasha Fierce persona in later years, the character became iconic among fans and critics alike, representing a pivotal moment in her artistic evolution and approach to stage presence.

Beyoncé has been described as a having a wide-ranging sex appeal, with music journalist Touré writing that since the release of Dangerously in Love, she has "become a crossover sex symbol". Offstage Beyoncé says that while she likes to dress sexily, her onstage dress "is absolutely for the stage." She has emphasized that her provocative performances are an artistic choice rather than a reflection of her personal life, maintaining a distinction between her public persona and private self. This boundary has allowed her to control her own narrative while continuing to push creative boundaries in her visual presentation and choreography.
sampled_sentence: Architecturally, the school has a Catholic character.
Atop the Main Building's gold dome is a golden statue of the Virgin Mary.
Immediately in front of the Main Building and facing it, is a copper statue of Christ with arms upraised with the legend "Venite Ad Me Omnes".
Next to the Main Building is the Basilica of the Sacred Heart.
The Basilica's stunning interior features ornate stained glass windows and soaring vaulted ceilings that inspire contemplation and prayer.
Throughout the campus, these religious monuments serve as constant reminders of the institution's spiritual mission and Catholic heritage.
Together, they create a distinctive architectural landscape that defines the university's identity and distinguishes it from secular institutions.
As at most other universities, Notre Dame's students run a number of news media outlets.
The nine student-run outlets include three newspapers, both a radio and television station, and several magazines and journals.
Begun as a one-page journal in September 1876, the Scholastic magazine is issued twice monthly and claims to be the oldest continuous collegiate publication in the United States.
The other magazine, The Juggler, is released twice a year and focuses on student literature and artwork.
The Dome yearbook is published annually.

The newspapers have varying publication interests, with The Observer published daily and mainly reporting university and other news, and staffed by students from both Notre Dame and Saint Mary's College.
The Scholastic also maintains a strong online presence, complementing its print editions with digital content that reaches a broader audience beyond the campus community.
Meanwhile, the university's radio station, WSND-FM, broadcasts student-produced programming throughout the academic year, offering aspiring journalists and broadcasters valuable hands-on experience.
The television station similarly provides students with opportunities to develop skills in video production and broadcast journalism.
Together, these media outlets serve as important platforms for student voices and perspectives, while also functioning as training grounds for those pursuing careers in communications and journalism.
The diversity of these publications reflects Notre Dame's commitment to fostering an engaged student body and supporting creative expression across multiple mediums.
The university is the major seat of the Congregation of Holy Cross (albeit not its official headquarters, which are in Rome).
Its main seminary, Moreau Seminary, is located on the campus across St. Joseph lake from the Main Building.
Old College, the oldest building on campus and located near the shore of St. Mary lake, houses undergraduate seminarians.
The presence of these religious institutions has profoundly shaped the university's character and mission for over 170 years.
Faculty and staff members, many of whom are members of the Congregation, have instilled a commitment to education in the service of others throughout the institution.
This Catholic and Holy Cross heritage remains central to the university's identity, influencing everything from its curriculum to its emphasis on social responsibility and ethical leadership.
The College of Engineering was established in 1920, however, early courses in civil and mechanical engineering were a part of the College of Science since the 1870s.

Today the college, housed in the Fitzpatrick, Cushing, and Stinson-Remick Halls of Engineering, includes five departments of study – aerospace and mechanical engineering, chemical and biomolecular engineering, civil engineering and geological sciences, computer science and engineering, and electrical engineering – with eight B.S. degrees offered.

The college has grown significantly since its formal establishment, expanding its facilities and academic offerings to meet the evolving demands of engineering education and industry, while maintaining its commitment to rigorous scholarship and practical innovation.
All of Notre Dame's undergraduate students are a part of one of the five undergraduate colleges at the school or are in the First Year of Studies program.
The First Year of Studies program was established in 1962 to guide incoming freshmen in their first year at the school before they have declared a major.
Each student is given an academic advisor from the program who helps them to choose classes that give them exposure to any major in which they are interested.
This structure allows first-year students to explore their academic interests broadly without committing to a specific college until they have a clearer sense of their career goals and passions.

Upon completing their freshman year, students then transition into one of Notre Dame's five undergraduate colleges—Arts and Letters, Science, Engineering, Business, or Architecture—where they complete their major requirements and earn their degree.
The university first offered graduate degrees, in the form of a Master of Arts (MA), in the 1854–1855 academic year.

The program expanded to include Master of Laws (LL.M.) and Master of Civil Engineering in its early stages of growth, before a formal graduate school education was developed with a thesis not required to receive the degrees.
This changed in 1924 with formal requirements developed for graduate degrees, including offering Doctorate (PhD) degrees.
Today each of the five colleges offer graduate education.
Most of the departments from the College of Arts and Letters offer PhD programs, while a professional Master of Divinity (M.Div.)
program also exists.
The graduate programs have continued to evolve and expand throughout the twentieth and twenty-first centuries, reflecting the university's commitment to advanced scholarship and research.

In recent decades, the institution has strengthened its graduate offerings by establishing interdisciplinary programs that bridge multiple colleges and departments, allowing students to pursue specialized fields of study.
Graduate enrollment has grown substantially, with the university now hosting thousands of graduate and professional students across all five colleges.

The graduate school maintains rigorous admission standards and provides comprehensive support services, including funding opportunities, mentorship programs, and access to state-of-the-art research facilities.
These developments have solidified the university's reputation as a leading institution for graduate education and research at the national and international levels.

The Joan B. Kroc Institute for International Peace Studies at the University of Notre Dame is dedicated to research, education and outreach on the causes of violent conflict and the conditions for sustainable peace.
It offers PhD, Master's, and undergraduate degrees in peace studies.
It was founded in 1986 through the donations of Joan B. Kroc, the widow of McDonald's owner Ray Kroc.

The institute's work spans multiple disciplines, including political science, economics, sociology, and theology, to provide students with comprehensive training in conflict resolution and peacebuilding.

Through its research centers, field programs, and partnerships with organizations around the world, the institute continues to develop innovative approaches to addressing global challenges and promoting justice and reconciliation.
The library system of the university is divided between the main library and each of the colleges and schools.
The main building is the 14-story Theodore M. Hesburgh Library, completed in 1963, which is the third building to house the main collection of books.
The library holds over three million volumes and serves as the intellectual heart of the campus, attracting scholars from around the world.
Its iconic architecture, featuring the famous "Touchdown Jesus" mural on its exterior, has made it one of the most recognizable academic buildings in the United States.
Notre Dame is known for its competitive admissions, with the incoming class enrolling in fall 2015 admitting 3,577 from a pool of 18,156 (19.7%).
The academic profile of the enrolled class continues to rate among the top 10 to 15 in the nation for national research universities.
The university practices a non-restrictive early action policy that allows admitted students to consider admission to Notre Dame as well as any other colleges to which they were accepted.
This approach reflects Notre Dame's commitment to attracting highly qualified students while maintaining flexibility in their college selection process.
The middle 50% of enrolled students typically score between 32-35 on the ACT and between 1450-1570 on the SAT, demonstrating the caliber of academic achievement among the incoming class.

Additionally, Notre Dame's holistic admissions process considers not only standardized test scores and GPA, but also extracurricular activities, leadership potential, and personal essays that reveal each applicant's character and values.
In 2015-2016, Notre Dame ranked 18th overall among "national universities" in the United States in U.S. News & World Report's Best Colleges 2016.
In 2014, USA Today ranked Notre Dame 10th overall for American universities based on data from College Factual.
Forbes.com's America's Best Colleges ranks Notre Dame 13th among colleges in the United States in 2015, 8th among Research Universities, and 1st in the Midwest.
U.S. News & World Report also lists Notre Dame Law School as 22nd overall.
BusinessWeek ranks Mendoza College of Business undergraduate school as 1st overall.
Notre Dame's engineering program has consistently earned recognition as one of the top in the nation, frequently appearing in the top 20 rankings across major publications.
The university's strong performance in these various rankings reflects its commitment to academic excellence across multiple disciplines and colleges.
Additionally, Notre Dame's graduate programs, particularly in fields such as theology, philosophy, and the sciences, have garnered national acclaim and attract students from around the world.
The institution's emphasis on research, coupled with its rigorous curriculum and accomplished faculty, continues to solidify its reputation as a leading American university.
These rankings underscore Notre Dame's position as a premier educational institution that balances rigorous academics with its distinctive Catholic mission.
Father Joseph Carrier, C.S.C. was Director of the Science Museum and the Library and Professor of Chemistry and Physics until 1874.
Carrier taught that scientific research and its promise for progress were not antagonistic to the ideals of intellectual and moral culture endorsed by the Church.

One of Carrier's students was Father John Augustine Zahm (1851–1921) who was made Professor and Co-Director of the Science Department at age 23 and by 1900 was a nationally prominent scientist and naturalist.
Zahm was active in the Catholic Summer School movement, which introduced Catholic laity to contemporary intellectual issues.

Zahm's influence extended beyond the classroom, as he became a vocal advocate for reconciling Catholic doctrine with modern scientific advancement during an era of considerable tension between faith and reason.

Through his prolific writing and public lectures, he sought to demonstrate that scientific inquiry and religious belief were compatible pursuits, thereby inspiring a generation of Catholic intellectuals to engage seriously with contemporary scholarship.

His work in founding and directing educational initiatives helped establish the Catholic Church's credibility in intellectual circles and provided a model for how religious institutions could foster rigorous scientific study.
In 1882, Albert Zahm (John Zahm's brother) built an early wind tunnel used to compare lift to drag of aeronautical models.
Around 1899, Professor Jerome Green became the first American to send a wireless message.
This groundbreaking achievement in wireless transmission marked a pivotal moment in the history of American telecommunications and demonstrated the nation's growing scientific prowess.
These innovations, though seemingly disparate, represented the broader spirit of experimentation and technological advancement that defined the turn of the century.
The Lobund Institute grew out of pioneering research in germ-free-life which began in 1928.
This area of research originated in a question posed by Pasteur as to whether animal life was possible without bacteria.
Though others had taken up this idea, their research was short lived and inconclusive.
Lobund was the first research organization to answer definitively, that such life is possible and that it can be prolonged through generations.
But the objective was not merely to answer Pasteur's question but also to produce the germ free animal as a new tool for biological and medical research.
The germ-free animal proved to be invaluable for investigating the role of microorganisms in disease, nutrition, and development.
Researchers could now isolate the effects of specific bacterial populations from the countless confounding variables present in conventional laboratory animals.
This breakthrough opened entirely new avenues for understanding how the microbiome influences host physiology and pathology.
The Institute's success in maintaining these sterile colonies across multiple generations established protocols that would become standard in research facilities worldwide.
By creating this controlled biological system, Lobund fundamentally transformed the landscape of experimental medicine and laid the groundwork for modern microbiome science.
The Review of Politics was founded in 1939 by Gurian, modeled after German Catholic journals.
It quickly emerged as part of an international Catholic intellectual revival, offering an alternative vision to positivist philosophy.
For 44 years, the Review was edited by Gurian, Matthew Fitzsimons, Frederick Crosson, and Thomas Stritch.
The journal's pages became a forum for rigorous philosophical debate, attracting contributions from prominent Catholic thinkers and scholars across Europe and America.
Through its commitment to integrating faith with reason, The Review of Politics established itself as a vital intellectual force that shaped Catholic thought throughout the twentieth century.
As of 2012[update] research continued in many fields.
The university president, John Jenkins, described his hope that Notre Dame would become "one of the pre–eminent research institutions in the world" in his inaugural address.

The university has many multi-disciplinary institutes devoted to research in varying fields, including the Medieval Institute, the Kellogg Institute for International Studies, the Kroc Institute for International Peace studies, and the Center for Social Concerns.
These institutes have attracted leading scholars and generated significant funding from both governmental and private sources to support their ambitious research agendas.

Furthermore, Notre Dame's commitment to research excellence has been complemented by substantial investments in state-of-the-art facilities and laboratories across campus to enable faculty and students to pursue cutting-edge investigations.
In 2014 the Notre Dame student body consisted of 12,179 students, with 8,448 undergraduates, 2,138 graduate and professional and 1,593 professional (Law, M.Div.,
Business, M.Ed.)
students.
Around 21–24% of students are children of alumni, and although 37% of students come from the Midwestern United States, the student body represents all 50 states and 100 countries.
As of March 2007[update] The Princeton Review ranked the school as the fifth highest 'dream school' for parents to send their children.

# Continuation

The university's commitment to geographic and international diversity reflects its mission as a global Catholic institution dedicated to intellectual development and moral formation.
Notre Dame's selective admissions process prioritizes academic excellence, with admitted students typically demonstrating exceptional standardized test scores and high school performance.

The presence of such a substantial legacy student population—nearly a quarter of the incoming class—underscores the university's deep-rooted traditions and the loyalty it inspires across generations of families.

Beyond academics, the student body engages in over 100 registered student organizations, fostering a vibrant campus culture that balances rigorous intellectual pursuit with community service and spiritual growth.
This diverse and accomplished student population contributes significantly to Notre Dame's reputation as one of America's premier Catholic universities.
About 80% of undergraduates and 20% of graduate students live on campus.
The majority of the graduate students on campus live in one of four graduate housing complexes on campus, while all on-campus undergraduates live in one of the 29 residence halls.
Because of the religious affiliation of the university, all residence halls are single-sex, with 15 male dorms and 14 female dorms.

The university maintains a visiting policy (known as parietal hours) for those students who live in dormitories, specifying times when members of the opposite sex are allowed to visit other students' dorm rooms; however, all residence halls have 24-hour social spaces for students regardless of gender.
These policies reflect the institution's commitment to maintaining a structured residential environment that aligns with its values while still fostering community and social interaction.

The parietal hours system has been a longstanding tradition, with most residence halls implementing visitation windows during afternoon and evening hours on weekends, while weekday access is typically more restricted.
Despite these guidelines, the residence life staff works to create an inclusive atmosphere by organizing co-ed events, study groups, and social gatherings in the common areas throughout the week.

This balance between traditional residential policies and modern student life has allowed the university to preserve its distinctive character while adapting to the social needs of its diverse student population.
The university is affiliated with the Congregation of Holy Cross (Latin: Congregatio a Sancta Cruce, abbreviated postnominals: "CSC").
While religious affiliation is not a criterion for admission, more than 93% of students identify as Christian, with over 80% of the total being Catholic.
Collectively, Catholic Mass is celebrated over 100 times per week on campus, and a large campus ministry program provides for the faith needs of the community.

There are multitudes of religious statues and artwork around campus, most prominent of which are the statue of Mary on the Main Building, the Notre Dame Grotto, and the Word of Life mural on Hesburgh Library depicting Christ as a teacher.
The university maintains its Catholic identity through various traditions and observances that have been woven into campus life for generations.
Student organizations dedicated to faith and spirituality number in the dozens, offering opportunities for spiritual growth, service, and community building among the student body.
The campus also hosts numerous annual events and retreats designed to deepen students' understanding of Catholic social teaching and their personal faith journeys.

This commitment to integrating faith with education reflects the university's founding mission and continues to shape the experiences of students, faculty, and staff throughout their time at the institution.
This Main Building, and the library collection, was entirely destroyed by a fire in April 1879, and the school closed immediately and students were sent home.
The university founder, Fr.
Sorin and the president at the time, the Rev. William Corby, immediately planned for the rebuilding of the structure that had housed virtually the entire University.
Construction was started on the 17th of May and by the incredible zeal of administrator and workers the building was completed before the fall semester of 1879.
The library collection was also rebuilt and stayed housed in the new Main Building for years afterwards.
The rapid reconstruction became a testament to the resilience and determination of the university community, inspiring both faculty and students to return with renewed commitment.
The new Main Building, constructed with improved fire safety measures, stood as a symbol of the institution's unwavering dedication to its educational mission.
This remarkable achievement of rebuilding within mere months earned the university considerable recognition throughout the academic world and strengthened its reputation.
The successful recovery from this devastating disaster ultimately proved that no single setback could diminish the university's resolve to provide quality education to its students.

In 1919 Father James Burns became president of Notre Dame, and in three years he produced an academic revolution that brought the school up to national standards by adopting the elective system and moving away from the university's traditional scholastic and classical emphasis.
By contrast, the Jesuit colleges, bastions of academic conservatism, were reluctant to move to a system of electives.
Their graduates were shut out of Harvard Law School for that reason.
This disparity in educational philosophy created a widening gap between Catholic and secular institutions during the early twentieth century.

The Jesuits' commitment to a fixed curriculum, while maintaining intellectual rigor in classical studies, increasingly appeared outdated to admissions officers at elite universities who valued specialized preparation.

Eventually, facing mounting pressure and the threat of diminished opportunities for their students, even the most tradition-bound Jesuit colleges would reluctantly begin to modernize their academic structures.
One of the main driving forces in the growth of the University was its football team, the Notre Dame Fighting Irish.
Knute Rockne became head coach in 1918.
Under Rockne, the Irish would post a record of 105 wins, 12 losses, and five ties.
During his 13 years the Irish won three national championships, had five undefeated seasons, won the Rose Bowl in 1925, and produced players such as George Gipp and the "Four Horsemen".

Rockne's success on the field translated into unprecedented popularity and prestige for the university, drawing students and donors from across the nation who wanted to be part of the winning tradition.
His innovative coaching methods and charismatic leadership transformed Notre Dame into a national powerhouse, establishing a football legacy that would endure for generations.
The combination of athletic excellence and academic reputation made Notre Dame one of the most sought-after universities in America during the 1920s and beyond.
The success of its football team made Notre Dame a household name.
The success of Note Dame reflected rising status of Irish Americans and Catholics in the 1920s.

Catholics rallied up around the team and listen to the games on the radio, especially when it knocked off the schools that symbolized the Protestant establishment in America — Harvard, Yale, Princeton, and Army.
Yet this role as high-profile flagship institution of Catholicism made it an easy target of anti-Catholicism.
The most remarkable episode of violence was the clash between Notre Dame students and the Ku Klux Klan in 1924.
Nativism and anti-Catholicism, especially when directed towards immigrants, were cornerstones of the KKK's rhetoric, and Notre Dame was seen as a symbol of the threat posed by the Catholic Church.
The confrontation erupted when Klan members attempted to hold a rally in nearby South Bend, Indiana, prompting hundreds of Notre Dame students to mobilize in opposition.
Armed with whatever weapons they could find, the students descended upon the gathering, determined to prevent the organization from spreading its hateful message in their community.
The resulting melee left several people injured and sent shockwaves through both the university and the broader American public.
Rather than condemning the students' actions outright, many Catholic newspapers and leaders celebrated their defense of their faith and institution.
The incident became a pivotal moment in demonstrating that Irish Catholic Americans would no longer passively accept discrimination and prejudice.
In many ways, the battle between Notre Dame and the Klan symbolized a larger struggle for acceptance and equality that would define Catholic immigrant communities throughout the twentieth century.
Holy Cross Father John Francis O'Hara was elected vice-president in 1933 and president of Notre Dame in 1934.

During his tenure at Notre Dame, he brought numerous refugee intellectuals to campus; he selected Frank H. Spearman, Jeremiah D. M. Ford, Irvin Abell, and Josephine Brownson for the Laetare Medal, instituted in 1883.
O'Hara strongly believed that the Fighting Irish football team could be an effective means to "acquaint the public with the ideals that dominate" Notre Dame.
He leveraged the university's growing athletic prominence to enhance its academic reputation and expand its influence beyond Catholic circles.

Through his strategic vision, O'Hara transformed Notre Dame into a nationally recognized institution that successfully balanced its commitment to rigorous scholarship with its celebrated football program.
The Rev. John J. Cavanaugh, C.S.C. served as president from 1946 to 1952.

Cavanaugh's legacy at Notre Dame in the post-war years was devoted to raising academic standards and reshaping the university administration to suit it to an enlarged educational mission and an expanded student body and stressing advanced studies and research at a time when Notre Dame quadrupled in student census, undergraduate enrollment increased by more than half, and graduate student enrollment grew fivefold.
Cavanaugh also established the Lobund Institute for Animal Studies and Notre Dame's Medieval Institute.
His leadership during this transformative period established Notre Dame as a major research university, positioning it among the nation's premier Catholic institutions of higher learning.

Through his strategic vision and administrative reforms, Cavanaugh successfully navigated the challenges of post-war expansion while maintaining the university's commitment to academic excellence and its Catholic identity.
The Rev. Theodore Hesburgh, C.S.C., (1917–2015) served as president for 35 years (1952–87) of dramatic transformations.

In that time the annual operating budget rose by a factor of 18 from $9.7 million to $176.6 million, and the endowment by a factor of 40 from $9 million to $350 million, and research funding by a factor of 20 from $735,000 to $15 million.

Under his visionary leadership, Notre Dame evolved from a regional Catholic institution into a world-class research university that attracted top scholars and students from across the globe, while maintaining its distinctive Catholic identity and commitment to educating leaders for a changing world.
Hesburgh is also credited with transforming the face of Notre Dame by making it a coeducational institution.

In the mid-1960s Notre Dame and Saint Mary's College developed a co-exchange program whereby several hundred students took classes not offered at their home institution, an arrangement that added undergraduate women to a campus that already had a few women in the graduate schools.
After extensive debate, merging with St. Mary's was rejected, primarily because of the differential in faculty qualifications and pay scales. "

In American college education," explained the Rev. Charles E. Sheedy, C.S.C., Notre Dame's Dean of Arts and Letters, "certain features formerly considered advantageous and enviable are now seen as anachronistic and out of place.... In this environment of diversity, the integration of the sexes is a normal and expected aspect, replacing separatism."
Instead, Notre Dame pursued a gradual path toward full coeducation, admitting its first class of undergraduate women in the fall of 1972.

This landmark decision reflected not only changing social attitudes but also Hesburgh's conviction that the university could better serve its educational mission by welcoming female students to its undergraduate programs.
The transition was carefully managed, with residential facilities and support services adjusted to accommodate the new student body.

Within a decade, women comprised a significant portion of Notre Dame's undergraduate enrollment, fundamentally reshaping the character and culture of the institution while maintaining its Catholic identity and academic standards.
In the 18 years under the presidency of Edward Malloy, C.S.C., (1987–2005), there was a rapid growth in the school's reputation, faculty, and resources.

He increased the faculty by more than 500 professors; the academic quality of the student body has improved dramatically, with the average SAT score rising from 1240 to 1360; the number of minority students more than doubled; the endowment grew from $350 million to more than $3 billion; the annual operating budget rose from $177 million to more than $650 million; and annual research funding improved from $15 million to more than $70 million.

During this transformative period, Malloy also oversaw the construction of numerous state-of-the-art academic buildings and residence halls, fundamentally reshaping the campus landscape and enhancing the university's physical infrastructure.
His strategic vision and fundraising prowess established Notre Dame as one of the nation's premier research institutions, solidifying its position among the top-tier universities in the country.
Since 2005, Notre Dame has been led by John I. Jenkins, C.S.C., the 17th president of the university.
Jenkins took over the position from Malloy on July 1, 2005.
Under his leadership, Jenkins has focused on strengthening the university's academic reputation and expanding its research initiatives across multiple disciplines.
He has also worked to enhance Notre Dame's global presence through international partnerships and increased opportunities for students to engage in service-oriented projects worldwide.
Because of its Catholic identity, a number of religious buildings stand on campus.
The Old College building has become one of two seminaries on campus run by the Congregation of Holy Cross.
The current Basilica of the Sacred Heart is located on the spot of Fr.
Sorin's original church, which became too small for the growing college.
It is built in French Revival style and it is decorated by stained glass windows imported directly from France.
The interior was painted by Luigi Gregori, an Italian painter invited by Fr.
Sorin to create murals depicting scenes from the life of Christ and the saints.
The basilica's dome is one of the most recognizable landmarks on campus, visible from nearly every corner of the grounds and serving as a symbol of the university's spiritual mission.
Its golden cross, which crowns the dome, has become an iconic image associated with the institution for generations of students and alumni.
The basilica continues to serve as the spiritual heart of the campus community, hosting daily masses, special liturgical celebrations, and important university events throughout the academic year.
In addition to its religious significance, the building stands as an architectural masterpiece that reflects the university's commitment to preserving its rich Catholic heritage.
A Science Hall was built in 1883 under the direction of Fr.
Zahm, but in 1950 it was converted to a student union building and named LaFortune Center, after Joseph LaFortune, an oil executive from Tulsa, Oklahoma.

Commonly known as "LaFortune" or "LaFun," it is a 4-story building of 83,000 square feet that provides the Notre Dame community with a meeting place for social, recreational, cultural, and educational activities.
The center features a variety of amenities including dining facilities, conference rooms, a bookstore, and recreational spaces designed to foster student engagement and community building.
Its central location on campus makes it one of the most frequently visited buildings at Notre Dame, serving as a hub where students, faculty, and visitors gather throughout the day.

Since its renovation, LaFortune has undergone several expansions and modernizations to meet the evolving needs of the university community and remain a vital gathering space for generations of Notre Dame students.
Since the construction of its oldest buildings, the university's physical plant has grown substantially.
Over the years 29 residence halls have been built to accommodate students and each has been constructed with its own chapel.
Many academic building were added together with a system of libraries, the most prominent of which is the Theodore Hesburgh Library, built in 1963 and today containing almost 4 million books.
Since 2004, several buildings have been added, including the DeBartolo Performing Arts Center, the Guglielmino Complex, and the Jordan Hall of Science.
Additionally, a new residence for men, Duncan Hall, was begun on March 8, 2007, and began accepting residents for the Fall 2008 semester.
Ryan Hall was completed and began housing undergraduate women in the fall of 2009.

A new engineering building, Stinson-Remick Hall, a new combination Center for Social Concerns/Institute for Church Life building, Geddes Hall, and a law school addition have recently been completed as well.
The university continues to invest in modern facilities that support both academic excellence and student life.
In recent years, the campus has undergone significant renovations to existing structures, updating laboratories, classrooms, and recreational spaces to meet contemporary educational standards.
The addition of green building technologies and sustainable design practices reflects the institution's commitment to environmental responsibility.
Campus planners have also prioritized the creation of more collaborative learning spaces that encourage interdisciplinary work among students and faculty.
These ongoing construction projects demonstrate the university's dedication to maintaining a world-class physical infrastructure that attracts top scholars and students from around the globe.
Looking forward, the institution remains committed to strategic expansion that will enhance both the academic mission and the overall quality of campus life.

The University of Notre Dame has made being a sustainability leader an integral part of its mission, creating the Office of Sustainability in 2008 to achieve a number of goals in the areas of power generation, design and construction, waste reduction, procurement, food services, transportation, and water.
As of 2012[update] four building construction projects were pursuing LEED-Certified status and three were pursuing LEED Silver.
Notre Dame's dining services sources 40% of its food locally and offers sustainably caught seafood as well as many organic, fair-trade, and vegan options.
The university has also committed to reducing its carbon footprint through renewable energy initiatives, including the installation of solar panels and geothermal heating systems across campus.

Additionally, Notre Dame has implemented comprehensive recycling and composting programs that divert thousands of tons of waste from landfills annually, while its transportation efforts focus on expanding electric vehicle charging stations and promoting the use of public transit among students and staff.
These multifaceted sustainability efforts demonstrate the institution's dedication to environmental stewardship and serving as a model for responsible campus management in higher education.
The university owns several centers around the world used for international studies and research, conferences abroad, and alumni support.
The university has had a presence in London, England, since 1968.
Since 1998, its London center has been based in the former United University Club at 1 Suffolk Street in Trafalgar Square.

The center serves as a hub for students and faculty conducting research in Europe while also hosting lectures and cultural events that strengthen ties between the institution and the local academic community.

Additionally, the university maintains similar centers in other major cities including Rome, Paris, and Beijing, each tailored to support discipline-specific programs and foster global scholarly exchange.
The College of Arts and Letters was established as the university's first college in 1842 with the first degrees given in 1849.
The university's first academic curriculum was modeled after the Jesuit Ratio Studiorum from Saint Louis University.
Throughout the nineteenth century, the college expanded its offerings to include modern languages, natural sciences, and historical studies, reflecting the evolving needs of its students and society.
By the early twentieth century, it had become the intellectual heart of the university, producing scholars, educators, and leaders who would shape the cultural and academic landscape of the nation.
The College of Science was established at the university in 1865 by president Father Patrick Dillon.
Dillon's scientific courses were six years of work, including higher-level mathematics courses.
The rigorous curriculum reflected Dillon's conviction that a comprehensive scientific education required both theoretical knowledge and practical application.

By the end of the nineteenth century, the College of Science had become one of the most respected programs in the region, attracting students from across the country who sought to master the disciplines of chemistry, physics, and natural history.
The School of Architecture was established in 1899, although degrees in architecture were first awarded by the university in 1898.
Today the school, housed in Bond Hall, offers a five-year undergraduate program leading to the Bachelor of Architecture degree.
All undergraduate students study the third year of the program in Rome.
The Rome program provides students with an immersive experience in one of the world's greatest architectural laboratories, where they can study classical design principles in their original context.
This unique opportunity allows architects-in-training to develop a deep understanding of historical and contemporary design while building lasting connections with their peers from around the globe.

The library system also includes branch libraries for Architecture, Chemistry & Physics, Engineering, Law, and Mathematics as well as information centers in the Mendoza College of Business, the Kellogg Institute for International Studies, the Joan B. Kroc Institute for International Peace Studies, and a slide library in O'Shaughnessy Hall.
A theology library was also opened in fall of 2015.
These specialized facilities ensure that students and faculty across diverse disciplines have access to resources tailored to their specific research and academic needs.

The university's commitment to maintaining subject-specific libraries reflects its recognition that different fields of study require distinct collections and specialized expertise to support scholarly excellence.
The rise of Hitler and other dictators in the 1930s forced numerous Catholic intellectuals to flee Europe; president John O'Hara brought many to Notre Dame.
From Germany came Anton-Hermann Chroust (1907–1982) in classics and law, and Waldemar Gurian a German Catholic intellectual of Jewish descent.
Positivism dominated American intellectual life in the 1920s onward but in marked contrast, Gurian received a German Catholic education and wrote his doctoral dissertation under Max Scheler.

Gurian's arrival at Notre Dame in 1936 introduced a philosophical counterweight to the prevailing empiricist trends, bringing with him the rich tradition of Catholic phenomenology and social thought that would profoundly influence the university's intellectual direction.

His presence, alongside other European scholars, helped establish Notre Dame as a refuge for displaced intellectuals and transformed it into a center where European Catholic philosophy could flourish and engage with American academic culture.
The University of Notre Dame du Lac (or simply Notre Dame /ˌnoʊtərˈdeɪm/ NOH-tər-DAYM) is a Catholic research university located adjacent to South Bend, Indiana, in the United States.
In French, Notre Dame du Lac means "Our Lady of the Lake" and refers to the university's patron saint, the Virgin Mary.

The university was founded in 1842 by the Congregation of Holy Cross, a French religious community, and has since grown into one of the most prestigious and selective universities in the country, known for its strong academic programs, particularly in business, engineering, and the sciences, as well as its prominent football tradition and distinctive Golden Dome, which serves as an iconic symbol of the institution.
Notre Dame rose to national prominence in the early 1900s for its Fighting Irish football team, especially under the guidance of the legendary coach Knute Rockne.
The university's athletic teams are members of the NCAA Division I and are known collectively as the Fighting Irish.

The football team, an Independent, has accumulated eleven consensus national championships, seven Heisman Trophy winners, 62 members in the College Football Hall of Fame and 13 members in the Pro Football Hall of Fame and is considered one of the most famed and successful college football teams in history.
Rockne's innovative coaching methods and strategic brilliance transformed Notre Dame into a powerhouse, establishing traditions and a winning culture that would endure for generations.

The program's success on the field became inseparable from the university's identity, attracting top talent from across the nation and cementing Notre Dame's place among college football's elite institutions.

Besides its prominence in sports, Notre Dame is also a large, four-year, highly residential research University, and is consistently ranked among the top twenty universities in the United States and as a major global university.
The undergraduate component of the university is organized into four colleges (Arts and Letters, Science, Engineering, Business) and the Architecture School.
The latter is known for teaching New Classical Architecture and for awarding the globally renowned annual Driehaus Architecture Prize.

Notre Dame's graduate program has more than 50 master's, doctoral and professional degree programs offered by the five schools, with the addition of the Notre Dame Law School and a MD-PhD program offered in combination with IU medical School.

The university's commitment to research excellence is reflected in its substantial annual research expenditures, which exceed $200 million and span disciplines from engineering and science to theology and the humanities.

Notre Dame's location in South Bend, Indiana, provides students with a vibrant campus community while maintaining proximity to major metropolitan areas, fostering a unique blend of collegiate life and access to broader intellectual and professional networks.
The university's Catholic identity remains central to its mission, emphasizing values of social responsibility, ethical leadership, and service to others alongside rigorous academic inquiry.

In 1842, the Bishop of Vincennes, Célestine Guynemer de la Hailandière, offered land to Father Edward Sorin of the Congregation of the Holy Cross, on the condition that he build a college in two years.
Fr.
Sorin arrived on the site with eight Holy Cross brothers from France and Ireland on November 26, 1842, and began the school using Father Stephen Badin's old log chapel.
The small band of religious men faced daunting challenges as they cleared the frozen wilderness and constructed temporary buildings to house students and faculty through the harsh Indiana winter.
Despite these hardships, Fr.
Sorin's determination and vision for the institution never wavered, and he would ultimately transform this remote frontier settlement into one of America's most renowned Catholic universities.
The first degrees from the college were awarded in 1849.
The university was expanded with new buildings to accommodate more students and faculty.
With each new president, new academic programs were offered and new buildings built to accommodate them.
By the turn of the twentieth century, the institution had grown from a modest liberal arts college into a comprehensive university offering advanced degrees across multiple disciplines.
The addition of professional schools in medicine, law, and engineering reflected the university's ambition to become a leading research institution.
This period of expansion transformed not only the physical campus but also solidified the university's reputation as a center of intellectual innovation and academic excellence.
The television station, NDtv, grew from one show in 2002 to a full 24-hour channel with original programming by September 2006.

WSND-FM serves the student body and larger South Bend community at 88.9 FM, offering students a chance to become involved in bringing classical music, fine arts and educational programming, and alternative rock to the airwaves.
The station has become a vital outlet for student talent and creativity, allowing aspiring broadcasters and musicians to gain hands-on experience in a professional radio environment.
Through its diverse programming schedule, WSND-FM has established itself as a unique voice in the local media landscape, bridging the gap between the university and the surrounding community.
The first phase of Eddy Street Commons, a $215 million development located adjacent to the University of Notre Dame campus and funded by the university, broke ground on June 3, 2008.

The Eddy Street Commons drew union protests when workers hired by the City of South Bend to construct the public parking garage picketed the private work site after a contractor hired non-union workers.
The dispute highlighted ongoing tensions in the construction industry regarding labor practices and worker representation in large-scale development projects in the region.
Notre Dame teams are known as the Fighting Irish.
They compete as a member of the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) Division I, primarily competing in the Atlantic Coast Conference (ACC) for all sports since the 2013–14 school year.
The Fighting Irish previously competed in the Horizon League from 1982-83 to 1985-86, and again from 1987-88 to 1994-95, and then in the Big East Conference through 2012–13.
This athletic tradition spans decades of competitive excellence across multiple conferences and divisions.
The university's move to the ACC marked a significant shift in its conference affiliation, aligning Notre Dame with other prestigious institutions on the national stage.

The Fighting Irish have maintained their distinctive identity and competitive spirit throughout these transitions, continuing to be recognized as one of college sports' most storied and respected programs.

Notre Dame's conference affiliations for all of its sports except football and fencing changed in July 2013 as a result of major conference realignment, and its fencing affiliation will change in July 2014.

The Irish left the Big East for the ACC during a prolonged period of instability in the Big East; while they maintain their football independence, they have committed to play five games per season against ACC opponents.

In ice hockey, the Irish were forced to find a new conference home after the Big Ten Conference's decision to add the sport in 2013–14 led to a cascade of conference moves that culminated in the dissolution of the school's former hockey home, the Central Collegiate Hockey Association, after the 2012–13 season.
Notre Dame moved its hockey team to Hockey East.
After Notre Dame joined the ACC, the conference announced it would add fencing as a sponsored sport beginning in the 2014–15 school year.

There are many theories behind the adoption of the athletics moniker but it is known that the Fighting Irish name was used in the early 1920s with respect to the football team and was popularized by alumnus Francis Wallace in his New York Daily News columns.
The nickname gained further prominence during the era of legendary coach Knute Rockne, whose teams became synonymous with the Fighting Irish identity.
By the mid-20th century, the moniker had become deeply embedded in Notre Dame's athletic culture and was adopted across all sports programs.
The university's leprechaun mascot, which debuted in the 1950s, complemented the Fighting Irish name and became an iconic symbol recognized nationwide.
Today, the Fighting Irish brand represents not only athletic excellence but also the university's Irish Catholic heritage and its tradition of resilience and competitive spirit.
The combination of the nickname, mascot, and storied athletic history has made Notre Dame one of the most recognizable and celebrated athletic programs in American sports.

On July 1, 2014, the University of Notre Dame and Under Armour reached an agreement in which Under Armour will provide uniforms, apparel,equipment, and monetary compensation to Notre Dame for 10 years.
This contract, worth almost $100 million, is the most lucrative in the history of the NCAA.
The university marching band plays at home games for most of the sports.
The partnership represented a significant shift in how major universities approach athletic sponsorships, elevating Notre Dame's brand visibility on a national and international scale.
