Under Armour's investment reflected the company's commitment to establishing itself as a premier outfitter for elite collegiate athletic programs.
The deal also provided Notre Dame with substantial resources to enhance its athletic facilities, recruit top talent, and strengthen its competitive position across multiple sports.
The Notre Dame football team has a long history, first beginning when the Michigan Wolverines football team brought football to Notre Dame in 1887 and played against a group of students.

In the long history since then, 13 Fighting Irish teams have won consensus national championships (although the university only claims 11), along with another nine teams being named national champion by at least one source.

The program's most recent national championship came in 1988, led by quarterback Tony Rice and head coach Lou Holtz, cementing Notre Dame's status as one of college football's most storied and successful franchises.
Throughout the decades, the Fighting Irish have produced countless All-Americans, NFL draft picks, and legendary coaches who have shaped not only the university's identity but also the sport itself.
George Gipp was the school's legendary football player during 1916–20.
He played semiprofessional baseball and smoked, drank, and gambled when not playing sports.
He was also humble, generous to the needy, and a man of integrity.
It was in 1928 that famed coach Knute Rockne used his final conversation with the dying Gipp to inspire the Notre Dame team to beat the Army team and "win one for the Gipper."
The 1940 film, Knute Rockne, All American, starred Pat O'Brien as Knute Rockne and Ronald Reagan as Gipp.
Today the team competes in Notre Dame Stadium, an 80,795-seat stadium on campus.
The current head coach is Brian Kelly, hired from the University of Cincinnati on December 11, 2009.
Under Kelly's leadership, the program has achieved consistent success, including multiple College Football Playoff appearances and several victories over ranked opponents.
The team maintains a rigorous schedule that includes traditional rivalries with schools such as USC, Michigan, and Boston College.
Notre Dame's football tradition extends beyond the field, as players are expected to uphold the university's academic standards alongside their athletic commitments.
The Fighting Irish have produced numerous professional athletes who have gone on to successful careers in the NFL and other sports leagues.
Fans and alumni remain deeply passionate about the program, making Notre Dame football an integral part of the university's identity and culture.
The combination of athletic excellence, historical prestige, and institutional values continues to attract top talent to the program year after year.
Football gameday traditions During home games, activities occur all around campus and different dorms decorate their halls with a traditional item (e.g. Zahm House's two-story banner).
Traditional activities begin at the stroke of midnight with the Drummers' Circle.
This tradition involves the drum line of the Band of the Fighting Irish and ushers in the rest of the festivities that will continue the rest of the gameday Saturday.
As dawn breaks over the quad, thousands of students gather for tailgating parties featuring food, games, and spirited conversations that build anticipation for the afternoon kickoff.
The energy culminates hours later when the entire student body floods into the stadium, their voices joining in a thunderous roar as the team takes the field.
The men's basketball team has over 1,600 wins, one of only 12 schools who have reached that mark, and have appeared in 28 NCAA tournaments.
Former player Austin Carr holds the record for most points scored in a single game of the tournament with 61.
Although the team has never won the NCAA Tournament, they were named by the Helms Athletic Foundation as national champions twice.
The team has orchestrated a number of upsets of number one ranked teams, the most notable of which was ending UCLA's record 88-game winning streak in 1974.
The team has beaten an additional eight number-one teams, and those nine wins rank second, to UCLA's 10, all-time in wins against the top team.
The team plays in newly renovated Purcell Pavilion (within the Edmund P. Joyce Center), which reopened for the beginning of the 2009–2010 season.

# Continuation

The facility has become a state-of-the-art venue that showcases the program's rich history and commitment to excellence.
With a seating capacity of over 9,600, the arena provides an intimate yet energetic atmosphere for both home games and special events.
The team's success has been built on a foundation of strong coaching leadership, most notably under the direction of legendary coach Digger Phelps, who led the program for 20 seasons.
In recent years, the basketball program has continued to compete at a high level in the ACC conference, consistently recruiting talented players from around the country.
The passionate fan base and supportive alumni network have been instrumental in maintaining the program's competitiveness and tradition of excellence.
Today, the men's basketball team remains one of the most storied programs in college sports, with aspirations of finally capturing an NCAA championship title.
The "Notre Dame Victory March" is the fight song for the University of Notre Dame.
It was written by two brothers who were Notre Dame graduates.
The Rev. Michael J. Shea, a 1904 graduate, wrote the music, and his brother, John F. Shea, who earned degrees in 1906 and 1908, wrote the original lyrics.
The lyrics were revised in the 1920s; it first appeared under the copyright of the University of Notre Dame in 1928.
The march has become an iconic symbol of Notre Dame athletics and school spirit, performed at virtually every football game and university event.
Its rousing melody and stirring words have inspired generations of students, alumni, and fans to rally behind the Fighting Irish.
The song's enduring popularity extends beyond the campus, as it has been adopted by various military bands and orchestras throughout the country.
Today, "Notre Dame Victory March" remains one of the most recognizable college fight songs in America, a testament to the Shea brothers' musical legacy.

In the film Knute Rockne, All American, Knute Rockne (played by Pat O'Brien) delivers the famous "Win one for the Gipper" speech, at which point the background music swells with the "Notre Dame Victory March".
George Gipp was played by Ronald Reagan, whose nickname "The Gipper" was derived from this role.
This scene was parodied in the movie Airplane!
where the comedic timing and absurdist humor turned the emotional climax into a laugh-out-loud moment that perfectly captured the film's irreverent style.
The parody became one of the most memorable jokes in the disaster comedy genre, cementing the original speech's place in both sports cinema and popular culture.
Notre Dame alumni work in various fields.
Alumni working in political fields include state governors, members of the United States Congress, and former United States Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice.
A notable alumnus of the College of Science is Medicine Nobel Prize winner Eric F. Wieschaus.
A number of university heads are alumni, including Notre Dame's current president, the Rev. John Jenkins.
Alumni have also made significant contributions to the business world, with graduates founding and leading major corporations across industries such as technology, finance, and manufacturing.
In the arts and entertainment sector, Notre Dame alumni have achieved prominence as acclaimed authors, filmmakers, and performers who have shaped American culture.

Additionally, many Notre Dame graduates have distinguished themselves in law, medicine, and other professional fields, serving as judges, hospital administrators, and leaders of prestigious institutions worldwide.
Beyoncé Giselle Knowles-Carter (/biːˈjɒnseɪ/ bee-YON-say) (born September 4, 1981) is an American singer, songwriter, record producer and actress.
Born and raised in Houston, Texas, she performed in various singing and dancing competitions as a child, and rose to fame in the late 1990s as lead singer of R&B girl-group Destiny's Child.
The group became one of the best-selling female groups of all time, with hit singles like "Say My Name" and "Survivor" that dominated the charts worldwide.
After Destiny's Child disbanded in 2004, Beyoncé launched a highly successful solo career, establishing herself as one of the most influential and commercially dominant artists of the 21st century.
Following the disbandment of Destiny's Child in June 2005, she released her second solo album, B'Day (2006), which contained hits "Déjà Vu", "Irreplaceable", and "Beautiful Liar".
Beyoncé also ventured into acting, with a Golden Globe-nominated performance in Dreamgirls (2006), and starring roles in The Pink Panther (2006) and Obsessed (2009).

Her marriage to rapper Jay Z and portrayal of Etta James in Cadillac Records (2008) influenced her third album, I Am... Sasha Fierce (2008), which saw the birth of her alter-ego Sasha Fierce and earned a record-setting six Grammy Awards in 2010, including Song of the Year for "Single Ladies (Put a Ring on It)".
The album's commercial success and critical acclaim solidified Beyoncé's status as a global superstar and established her as one of the most influential artists of her generation.

Following this triumph, she continued to push creative boundaries with increasingly ambitious projects that would redefine her artistic legacy and expand her influence across music, film, and popular culture.
A self-described "modern-day feminist", Beyoncé creates songs that are often characterized by themes of love, relationships, and monogamy, as well as female sexuality and empowerment.
On stage, her dynamic, highly choreographed performances have led to critics hailing her as one of the best entertainers in contemporary popular music.

Throughout a career spanning 19 years, she has sold over 118 million records as a solo artist, and a further 60 million with Destiny's Child, making her one of the best-selling music artists of all time.
She has won 20 Grammy Awards and is the most nominated woman in the award's history.
Her influence extends beyond music into fashion, film, and social activism, where she has consistently used her platform to advocate for Black excellence and social justice.
Beyoncé's visual albums, particularly "Lemonade" and "Renaissance," have redefined how artists approach storytelling and album releases in the streaming era.
Her business acumen has been equally impressive, as she has built a multimillion-dollar empire through her entertainment ventures, including her clothing line and production company.

From her early days in Destiny's Child to her current status as a global icon, Beyoncé has continuously evolved as an artist while maintaining an unwavering commitment to artistic integrity and social consciousness.
Beyoncé Giselle Knowles was born in Houston, Texas, to Celestine Ann "Tina" Knowles (née Beyincé), a hairdresser and salon owner, and Mathew Knowles, a Xerox sales manager.
Beyoncé's name is a tribute to her mother's maiden name.
Beyoncé's younger sister Solange is also a singer and a former member of Destiny's Child.
Growing up in Houston's Third Ward, Beyoncé was exposed to music and performance from an early age, with her mother encouraging her artistic talents.
She began taking dance classes and performing in local talent shows as a child, quickly demonstrating the star quality that would later captivate millions.

Her father eventually became her manager, playing a crucial role in shaping her early career and helping to launch her into the spotlight as part of what would become one of the most successful girl groups of all time.
Beyoncé attended St. Mary's Elementary School in Fredericksburg, Texas, where she enrolled in dance classes.
Her singing talent was discovered when dance instructor Darlette Johnson began humming a song and she finished it, able to hit the high-pitched notes.
Beyoncé's interest in music and performing continued after winning a school talent show at age seven, singing John Lennon's "Imagine" to beat 15/16-year-olds.
This early victory sparked a passion that would define her childhood, leading her to participate in numerous local competitions and performances throughout Texas.
By the time she reached her preteen years, Beyoncé had already developed a stage presence and vocal control that set her apart from her peers.

Her parents recognized her exceptional talent and began actively supporting her musical education, enrolling her in voice lessons and seeking out opportunities for her to perform and develop her craft.
At age eight, Beyoncé and childhood friend Kelly Rowland met LaTavia Roberson while in an audition for an all-girl entertainment group.
They were placed into a group with three other girls as Girl's Tyme, and rapped and danced on the talent show circuit in Houston.
After seeing the group, R&B producer Arne Frager brought them to his Northern California studio and placed them in Star Search, the largest talent show on national TV at the time.
Girl's Tyme failed to win, and Beyoncé later said the song they performed was not good.
In 1995 Beyoncé's father resigned from his job to manage the group.
The move reduced Beyoncé's family's income by half, and her parents were forced to move into separated apartments.
Despite the financial hardship, Mathew Knowles's management proved instrumental in reshaping the group's trajectory.
He implemented rigorous training regimens, refined their choreography, and worked tirelessly to secure better performance opportunities.
Over the next few years, the group's lineup underwent several changes, with members rotating in and out until the core configuration solidified.
By the late 1990s, Girl's Tyme had evolved significantly from their early days on the talent circuit, developing a more polished sound and professional image.
The sacrifices Beyoncé's family made during this period would ultimately lay the foundation for what would become one of the most successful girl groups in music history.
The group changed their name to Destiny's Child in 1996, based upon a passage in the Book of Isaiah.
In 1997, Destiny's Child released their major label debut song "Killing Time" on the soundtrack to the 1997 film, Men in Black.
The following year, the group released their self-titled debut album, scoring their first major hit "No, No, No".

The album established the group as a viable act in the music industry, with moderate sales and winning the group three Soul Train Lady of Soul Awards for Best R&B/Soul Album of the Year, Best R&B/Soul or Rap New Artist, and Best R&B/Soul Single for "No, No, No".
The group released their multi-platinum second album The Writing's on the Wall in 1999.

The album became a massive commercial and critical success, spawning several hit singles including "Bills, Bills, Bills" and "Jumpin', Jumpin'" that dominated radio airwaves and music charts worldwide. "
Bills, Bills, Bills" marked a turning point for the group, becoming their first number-one single on the Billboard Hot 100 and establishing them as mainstream superstars.

The Writing's on the Wall solidified Destiny's Child's position as one of the leading girl groups of the late 1990s, earning multiple Grammy nominations and platinum certifications across numerous countries.
By 2000, the group had become a cultural phenomenon, setting the stage for their continued dominance throughout the new decade.
LeToya Luckett and Roberson became unhappy with Mathew's managing of the band and eventually were replaced by Farrah Franklin and Michelle Williams.
Beyoncé experienced depression following the split with Luckett and Roberson after being publicly blamed by the media, critics, and blogs for its cause.
Her long-standing boyfriend left her at this time.
The depression was so severe it lasted for a couple of years, during which she occasionally kept herself in her bedroom for days and refused to eat anything.
During this dark period, Beyoncé's family became increasingly concerned about her wellbeing, with her mother Tina urging her to seek professional help and lean on those closest to her.
Despite her personal struggles, she channeled much of her pain into songwriting and musical expression, which would later emerge as some of her most emotionally resonant work.
Gradually, with the support of her family and renewed focus on her career with the reformed group, she began to emerge from the depths of her depression and rebuild her confidence.
The remaining band members recorded "Independent Women Part I", which appeared on the soundtrack to the 2000 film, Charlie's Angels.
It became their best-charting single, topping the U.S. Billboard Hot 100 chart for eleven consecutive weeks.

In early 2001, while Destiny's Child was completing their third album, Beyoncé landed a major role in the MTV made-for-television film, Carmen: A Hip Hopera, starring alongside American actor Mekhi Phifer.
Set in Philadelphia, the film is a modern interpretation of the 19th century opera Carmen by French composer Georges Bizet.
The performance showcased Beyoncé's versatility as an entertainer and introduced her to audiences who may not have been familiar with her work in Destiny's Child.
Her portrayal of the titular character Carmen was widely praised by critics, who noted her natural charisma and stage presence on screen.
This solo project marked an important milestone in her career, establishing her as a multifaceted artist capable of succeeding beyond the confines of her girl group.
The experience would prove invaluable as she began to explore her potential as a solo performer, setting the stage for her eventual transition to a successful independent career.

In July 2002, Beyoncé continued her acting career playing Foxxy Cleopatra alongside Mike Myers in the comedy film, Austin Powers in Goldmember, which spent its first weekend atop the US box office and grossed $73 million.
Beyoncé released "Work It Out" as the lead single from its soundtrack album which entered the top ten in the UK, Norway, and Belgium.
In 2003, Beyoncé starred opposite Cuba Gooding, Jr., in the musical comedy The Fighting Temptations as Lilly, a single mother whom Gooding's character falls in love with.
The film received positive reviews for its performances and energetic musical numbers, showcasing Beyoncé's growing confidence as an actress.
Following the success of these film roles, she began to balance her solo music career more deliberately with her acting ambitions, establishing herself as a multi-talented entertainer.
Beyoncé's first solo recording was a feature on Jay Z's "'03 Bonnie & Clyde" that was released in October 2002, peaking at number four on the U.S. Billboard Hot 100 chart.
Her first solo album Dangerously in Love was released on June 24, 2003, after Michelle Williams and Kelly Rowland had released their solo efforts.
The album sold 317,000 copies in its first week, debuted atop the Billboard 200, and has since sold 11 million copies worldwide.
The album's success was driven by hit singles including "Crazy in Love," which featured Jay Z and became one of the biggest songs of 2003, and "Baby Boy," both of which topped the Billboard Hot 100.
Dangerously in Love earned Beyoncé five Grammy Awards at the 2004 Grammy Awards ceremony, establishing her as a formidable solo artist in her own right.
The album's critical and commercial triumph marked a significant turning point in her career, solidifying her transition from Destiny's Child member to international superstar.
In November 2003, she embarked on the Dangerously in Love Tour in Europe and later toured alongside Missy Elliott and Alicia Keys for the Verizon Ladies First Tour in North America.
On February 1, 2004, Beyoncé performed the American national anthem at Super Bowl XXXVIII, at the Reliant Stadium in Houston, Texas.
After the release of Dangerously in Love, Beyoncé had planned to produce a follow-up album using several of the left-over tracks.
However, this was put on hold so she could concentrate on recording Destiny Fulfilled, the final studio album by Destiny's Child.
The album was released in November 2004 and marked a significant moment in the group's history, as it would be their last project together before an indefinite hiatus.
Destiny's Child embarked on a farewell tour to support the album, allowing fans around the world to celebrate the group's iconic legacy and contributions to music.
Meanwhile, Beyoncé began laying the groundwork for her solo career, preparing to establish herself as a formidable force in the industry independent of the group that had launched her to fame.
Beyoncé's second solo album B'Day was released on September 5, 2006, in the US, to coincide with her twenty-fifth birthday.
It sold 541,000 copies in its first week and debuted atop the Billboard 200, becoming Beyoncé's second consecutive number-one album in the United States.
The album's lead single "Déjà Vu", featuring Jay Z, reached the top five on the Billboard Hot 100 chart.
The album showcased a more dance-oriented and edgy sound compared to her debut, with production from Timbaland and The Neptunes that gave B'Day a contemporary edge.

Critical reception was generally positive, with reviewers praising Beyoncé's vocal versatility and the album's infectious production, though some felt it lacked the cohesive narrative of her previous work.
Her first acting role of 2006 was in the comedy film The Pink Panther starring opposite Steve Martin, grossing $158.8 million at the box office worldwide.
Her second film Dreamgirls, the film version of the 1981 Broadway musical loosely based on The Supremes, received acclaim from critics and grossed $154 million internationally.
In it, she starred opposite Jennifer Hudson, Jamie Foxx, and Eddie Murphy playing a pop singer based on Diana Ross.
The film earned her a Golden Globe nomination and solidified her status as a leading actress in Hollywood.
Her performance was praised for its emotional depth and vocal prowess, showcasing her ability to carry a major musical drama.
By the end of 2006, she had established herself as one of the year's most bankable and critically respected stars.

On April 4, 2008, Beyoncé married Jay Z. She publicly revealed their marriage in a video montage at the listening party for her third studio album, I Am... Sasha Fierce, in Manhattan's Sony Club on October 22, 2008.
I Am... Sasha Fierce was released on November 18, 2008 in the United States.

The album formally introduces Beyoncé's alter ego Sasha Fierce, conceived during the making of her 2003 single "Crazy in Love", selling 482,000 copies in its first week, debuting atop the Billboard 200, and giving Beyoncé her third consecutive number-one album in the US.
The album featured the number-one song "Single Ladies (Put a Ring on It)" and the top-five songs "If I Were a Boy" and "Halo".

Achieving the accomplishment of becoming her longest-running Hot 100 single in her career, "Halo"'s success in the US helped Beyoncé attain more top-ten singles on the list than any other woman during the 2000s.
It also included the successful "Sweet Dreams", and singles "Diva", "Ego", "Broken-Hearted Girl" and "Video Phone".

The album's commercial success extended internationally, with I Am... Sasha Fierce becoming a global phenomenon and solidifying Beyoncé's status as one of the most commercially successful artists of her generation.
Critics praised the album's ambitious production and diverse sonic palette, which blended pop, R&B, and dance influences across its twenty-seven tracks.
The project demonstrated Beyoncé's artistic growth and willingness to experiment with different personas and musical styles, earning multiple Grammy Award nominations.
In support of the album, Beyoncé embarked on the I Am... Tour, which became one of the highest-grossing concert tours by a female artist at that time.
The success of I Am... Sasha Fierce established a new peak in her career, setting the stage for continued dominance in the music industry throughout the following decade.
Beyoncé further expanded her acting career, starring as blues singer Etta James in the 2008 musical biopic, Cadillac Records.

Her performance in the film received praise from critics, and she garnered several nominations for her portrayal of James, including a Satellite Award nomination for Best Supporting Actress, and a NAACP Image Award nomination for Outstanding Supporting Actress.
Beyoncé donated her entire salary from the film to Phoenix House, an organization of rehabilitation centers for heroin addicts around the country.
On January 20, 2009, Beyoncé performed James' "At Last" at the First Couple's first inaugural ball.
The iconic performance became a memorable moment in presidential history, watched by millions of viewers who tuned in to witness the historic inauguration.
Following this success, Beyoncé continued to balance her music career with selective acting roles, choosing projects that aligned with her artistic vision and social values.
Her work in Cadillac Records demonstrated her ability to embody complex historical figures and deliver nuanced dramatic performances beyond her established reputation as a recording artist.
This period solidified her status as a multifaceted entertainer capable of commanding respect across both the music and film industries.

At the 52nd Annual Grammy Awards, Beyoncé received ten nominations, including Album of the Year for I Am... Sasha Fierce, Record of the Year for "Halo", and Song of the Year for "Single Ladies (Put a Ring on It)", among others.
She tied with Lauryn Hill for most Grammy nominations in a single year by a female artist.
In 2010, Beyoncé was featured on Lady Gaga's single "Telephone" and its music video.
The collaboration became an instant hit, showcasing both artists' distinctive styles and generating millions of views on YouTube.
That same year, Beyoncé also embarked on her highly successful "I Am... World Tour," which became one of the highest-grossing concert tours by a female artist at that time.
Beyoncé announced a hiatus from her music career in January 2010, heeding her mother's advice, "to live life, to be inspired by things again".
During the break she and her father parted ways as business partners.
The singer used this time away from the spotlight to focus on personal growth and spent time traveling and exploring new creative inspirations that would eventually influence her later work.
She also concentrated on her marriage to Jay-Z, whom she had married in 2008, and the couple began planning for their future family together.
In 2011, documents obtained by WikiLeaks revealed that Beyoncé was one of many entertainers who performed for the family of Libyan ruler Muammar Gaddafi.

Rolling Stone reported that the music industry was urging them to return the money they earned for the concerts; a spokesperson for Beyoncé later confirmed to The Huffington Post that she donated the money to the Clinton Bush Haiti Fund.

Following the donation, Beyoncé faced mixed reactions from critics who questioned whether the gesture fully addressed the ethical concerns surrounding her initial performance for a regime with a documented history of human rights abuses.
Her fourth studio album 4 was released on June 28, 2011 in the US.
4 sold 310,000 copies in its first week and debuted atop the Billboard 200 chart, giving Beyoncé her fourth consecutive number-one album in the US.
The album was preceded by two of its singles "Run the World (Girls)" and "Best Thing I Never Had", which both attained moderate success.
The fourth single "Love on Top" was a commercial success in the US.
The album showcased a more experimental and electronic sound compared to her previous work, with production from notable collaborators including Kanye West, Timbaland, and The Dream.
Critics praised Beyoncé's versatility and vocal performance throughout the record, though some felt the album lacked a cohesive narrative.
4 would go on to become one of the best-selling albums of the decade, cementing Beyoncé's status as one of the most commercially successful artists of her generation.
On January 7, 2012, Beyoncé gave birth to her first child, a daughter, Blue Ivy Carter, at Lenox Hill Hospital in New York.
The arrival of her daughter marked a significant milestone in Beyoncé's life, as she balanced her role as a new mother with her continued dominance in the music industry.
In January 2013, Destiny's Child released Love Songs, a compilation album of the romance-themed songs from their previous albums and a newly recorded track, "Nuclear".

Beyoncé performed the American national anthem singing along with a pre-recorded track at President Obama's second inauguration in Washington, D.C. The following month, Beyoncé performed at the Super Bowl XLVII halftime show, held at the Mercedes-Benz Superdome in New Orleans.
The performance stands as the second most tweeted about moment in history at 268,000 tweets per minute.
At the 55th Annual Grammy Awards, Beyoncé won for Best Traditional R&B Performance for "Love on Top".
Following her successful performances, Beyoncé began working on her fifth studio album, which would mark a significant departure from her previous work.
The album, titled "Beyoncé," was released on December 13, 2013, as a surprise visual album with no prior announcement or traditional marketing campaign.
This unconventional release strategy generated massive buzz and broke multiple streaming records within hours of its debut.

The self-titled album showcased a more experimental sound and featured collaborations with artists such as Jay-Z, Drake, and Frank Ocean, solidifying Beyoncé's position as one of the most innovative artists of her generation.
Beyoncé embarked on The Mrs. Carter Show World Tour on April 15 in Belgrade, Serbia; the tour included 132 dates that ran through to March 2014.
It became the most successful tour of her career and one of the most-successful tours of all time.
In May, Beyoncé's cover of Amy Winehouse's "Back to Black" with André 3000 on The Great Gatsby soundtrack was released.
The track received widespread critical acclaim for its sultry reinterpretation of the classic soul song, showcasing Beyoncé's versatility as an artist.
Following the success of both the tour and soundtrack contribution, she continued to solidify her status as one of the most influential entertainers of her generation.
On December 13, 2013, Beyoncé unexpectedly released her eponymous fifth studio album on the iTunes Store without any prior announcement or promotion.
The album debuted atop the Billboard 200 chart, giving Beyoncé her fifth consecutive number-one album in the US.
This made her the first woman in the chart's history to have her first five studio albums debut at number one.

Beyoncé received critical acclaim and commercial success, selling one million digital copies worldwide in six days; The New York Times noted the album's unconventional, unexpected release as significant.
Musically an electro-R&B album, it concerns darker themes previously unexplored in her work, such as "bulimia, postnatal depression [and] the fears and insecurities of marriage and motherhood".
The single "Drunk in Love", featuring Jay Z, peaked at number two on the Billboard Hot 100 chart.
The album's visual component, featuring an accompanying visual album with music videos for each track, revolutionized how artists could present their work in the streaming era.
Critics praised the production work of artists like The-Dream, Timbaland, and Ryan Tedder, who crafted sonically adventurous soundscapes that complemented Beyoncé's vocal performances.
The album's introspective nature marked a significant departure from the celebratory tone of her previous work, offering fans a glimpse into her private struggles and vulnerabilities.
Other notable tracks from the album, including "Pretty Hurts" and "Formation," became cultural touchstones that sparked conversations about identity, self-image, and social issues.
The surprise release strategy subsequently influenced how major artists approached album launches, with many attempting their own unannounced drops in the years following.
Beyoncé's fifth album ultimately solidified her status as one of music's most innovative and influential artists of her generation.

At the 57th Annual Grammy Awards in February 2015, Beyoncé was nominated for six awards, ultimately winning three: Best R&B Performance and Best R&B Song for "Drunk in Love", and Best Surround Sound Album for Beyoncé.
She was nominated for Album of the Year but the award was won by Beck for his Morning Phase album.

In August, the cover of the September issue of Vogue magazine was unveiled online, Beyoncé as the cover star, becoming the first African-American artist and third African-American woman in general to cover the September issue.
She headlined the 2015 Made in America festival in early September and also the Global Citizen Festival later that month.
The performances showcased her artistic evolution and cemented her status as one of the most influential entertainers of the era.

Throughout the remainder of 2015, Beyoncé continued to expand her creative endeavors, collaborating with other artists and working on new material that would eventually define the next chapter of her career.
Her dominance across music, fashion, and cultural conversations made it clear that her impact extended far beyond the recording industry itself.
On February 6, 2016, one day before her performance at the Super Bowl, Beyoncé released a new single exclusively on music streaming service Tidal called "Formation".
Beyoncé is believed to have first started a relationship with Jay Z after a collaboration on "'03 Bonnie & Clyde", which appeared on his seventh album The Blueprint 2: The Gift & The Curse (2002).
Beyoncé appeared as Jay Z's girlfriend in the music video for the song, which would further fuel speculation of their relationship.
On April 4, 2008, Beyoncé and Jay Z were married without publicity.
As of April 2014, the couple have sold a combined 300 million records together.
The two music superstars kept their marriage largely private for the first few years, rarely making public appearances together or discussing their relationship in interviews.
However, they began to collaborate more openly on music, with Jay Z featuring on Beyoncé's hit single "Drunk in Love" in 2013, which became a massive commercial and critical success.
Their partnership extended beyond music into business ventures, as they became one of the most influential power couples in the entertainment industry.
Together, they have continued to break records and dominate charts while maintaining a relatively guarded personal life away from the spotlight.

In August, the couple attended the 2011 MTV Video Music Awards, at which Beyoncé performed "Love on Top" and started the performance saying "Tonight I want you to stand up on your feet, I want you to feel the love that's growing inside of me".
At the end of the performance, she dropped her microphone, unbuttoned her blazer and rubbed her stomach, confirming her pregnancy she had alluded to earlier in the evening.

The announcement sent shockwaves through the crowd and instantly became one of the most memorable moments in VMAs history, with the gesture sparking countless headlines and celebratory reactions from fans around the world.
On January 7, 2012, Beyoncé gave birth to a daughter, Blue Ivy Carter, at Lenox Hill Hospital in New York under heavy security.
Two days later, Jay Z released "Glory", a song dedicated to their child, on his website Lifeandtimes.com.
The song detailed the couple's pregnancy struggles, including a miscarriage Beyoncé suffered before becoming pregnant with Blue Ivy.
The emotional track resonated deeply with fans and critics alike, who praised Jay Z's vulnerability and the couple's willingness to share such a personal journey. "
Glory" became a defining moment in their relationship, showcasing how they transformed their pain into art that connected with millions of people worldwide.
Beyoncé and husband Jay Z are friends with President Barack Obama and First Lady Michelle Obama.
She performed "America the Beautiful" at the 2009 presidential inauguration, as well as "At Last" during the first inaugural dance at the Neighborhood Ball two days later.
Beyoncé and Jay Z held a fundraiser at the latter's 40/40 Club in Manhattan for Obama's 2012 presidential campaign which raised $4 million.
Beyoncé uploaded pictures of her paper ballot on Tumblr, confirming she had voted in support for the Democratic Party and to encourage others to do so.
The couple's political engagement extended beyond financial contributions, as they became vocal advocates for various social justice causes throughout the 2010s.

Beyoncé's visual album "Lemonade" in 2016 featured imagery and themes that resonated with Black Lives Matter activists, while Jay Z's Roc Nation label began investing in criminal justice reform initiatives.

Their friendship with the Obamas remained strong even after leaving the White House, with the former president and first lady attending several of Beyoncé's major performances and events over the years.
In an interview published by Vogue in April 2013, Beyoncé was asked if she considers herself a feminist, to which she said, "that word can be very extreme... But I guess I am a modern-day feminist.
I do believe in equality".
She elaborated that feminism, for her, meant having the freedom to make choices—whether that meant pursuing a career, starting a family, or both—without judgment or limitation.
Beyoncé emphasized that she wanted her daughter Blue Ivy to grow up understanding that women deserved the same opportunities and respect as men in every aspect of life.

In 2015 Beyoncé signed an open letter which the ONE Campaign had been collecting signatures for; the letter was addressed to Angela Merkel and Nkosazana Dlamini-Zuma, urging them to focus on women as they serve as the head of the G7 in Germany and the AU in South Africa respectively, which will start to set the priorities in development funding before a main UN summit in September 2015 that will establish new development goals for the generation.
Following the death of Freddie Gray, Beyoncé and Jay-Z, among other notable figures, met with his family.

They offered their condolences and discussed ways to support the Gray family during their time of grief, while also pledging their commitment to advocacy work around issues of police accountability and racial justice.

Forbes magazine began reporting on Beyoncé's earnings in 2008, calculating that the $80 million earned between June 2007 to June 2008, for her music, tour, films and clothing line made her the world's best-paid music personality at the time, above Madonna and Celine Dion.
They placed her fourth on the Celebrity 100 list in 2009 and ninth on the "Most Powerful Women in the World" list in 2010.
The following year, Forbes placed her eighth on the "Best-Paid Celebrities Under 30" list, having earned $35 million in the past year for her clothing line and endorsement deals.

In 2012, Forbes placed Beyoncé at number 16 on the Celebrity 100 list, twelve places lower than three years ago yet still having earned $40 million in the past year for her album 4, clothing line and endorsement deals.
In the same year, Beyoncé and Jay Z placed at number one on the "World's Highest-Paid Celebrity Couples", for collectively earning $78 million.
The couple made it into the previous year's Guinness World Records as the "highest-earning power couple" for collectively earning $122 million in 2009.
For the years 2009 to 2011, Beyoncé earned an average of $70 million per year, and earned $40 million in 2012.

Her financial success continued to grow exponentially throughout the following decade, with her 2013 self-titled visual album marking a significant turning point in her career trajectory and commercial dominance.

The surprise release strategy and innovative multimedia approach generated unprecedented streaming numbers and album sales, further solidifying her position as one of the entertainment industry's most bankable stars.
By 2014, Beyoncé's net worth had climbed substantially, bolstered by her Formation World Tour, which became one of the highest-grossing tours by a female artist in history.
Forbes consistently ranked her among the top-earning celebrities globally, recognizing not only her musical talent but also her shrewd business acumen and ability to diversify her income streams.

Her partnership with Jay Z extended beyond their personal relationship into professional ventures, including the music streaming service Tidal, which demonstrated their ambition to reshape the digital music landscape.

As she entered her fourth decade, Beyoncé's influence transcended mere financial metrics, establishing her as a cultural icon whose economic impact reflected broader shifts in how artists could maintain control over their work and maximize their earning potential.
Beyoncé's vocal range spans four octaves.
Jody Rosen highlights her tone and timbre as particularly distinctive, describing her voice as "one of the most compelling instruments in popular music".
While another critic says she is a "Vocal acrobat, being able to sing long and complex melismas and vocal runs effortlessly, and in key.
Her vocal abilities mean she is identified as the centerpiece of Destiny's Child.
Her mastery of various vocal techniques allows her to seamlessly transition between powerful belting and delicate, nuanced phrasing.
Critics and fans alike have noted her ability to adapt her voice across multiple genres, from R&B and hip-hop to soul and pop, while maintaining her signature sound.
This versatility has made her one of the most influential vocalists of her generation, inspiring countless artists to push the boundaries of their own vocal capabilities.
Her technical proficiency, combined with her emotional depth and artistic interpretation, solidifies her legacy as one of the greatest singers in music history.
Beyoncé's music is generally R&B, but she also incorporates pop, soul and funk into her songs.
4 demonstrated Beyoncé's exploration of 90s-style R&B, as well as further use of soul and hip hop than compared to previous releases.
The album showcased her versatility as an artist and solidified her status as a trendsetter in the music industry.
Critics praised her willingness to experiment with different sounds while maintaining the sophisticated vocal delivery and production quality that her fans had come to expect.
She has received co-writing credits for most of the songs recorded with Destiny's Child and her solo efforts.

Her early songs were personally driven and female-empowerment themed compositions like "Independent Women" and "Survivor", but after the start of her relationship with Jay Z she transitioned to more man-tending anthems such as "Cater 2 U".
This shift reflected her evolving perspectives on love and partnership, showing a more vulnerable and domestic side of her artistry.
Over time, she would continue to balance both themes, crafting a diverse catalog that spoke to different chapters of her personal and professional journey.

In 2001, she became the first African-American woman and second woman songwriter to win the Pop Songwriter of the Year award at the American Society of Composers, Authors, and Publishers Pop Music Awards.
Beyoncé was the third woman to have writing credits on three number one songs ("Irreplaceable", "Grillz" and "Check on It") in the same year, after Carole King in 1971 and Mariah Carey in 1991.
She is tied with American songwriter Diane Warren at third with nine songwriting credits on number-one singles. (

Her contributions to popular music have been recognized across multiple genres, from R&B to hip-hop to pop, establishing her as one of the most versatile and influential songwriters of her generation.
These achievements solidified her position not just as a performer, but as a creative force behind some of the biggest hits of the 2000s.
Her songwriting prowess would continue to evolve throughout her career, earning her numerous Grammy Awards and further cementing her legacy in music history.
Beyoncé names Michael Jackson as her major musical influence.
Aged five, Beyoncé attended her first ever concert where Jackson performed and she claims to have realised her purpose.
When she presented him with a tribute award at the World Music Awards in 2006, Beyoncé said, "if it wasn't for Michael Jackson, I would never ever have performed."
She has frequently cited his innovative approach to choreography, music videos, and overall artistry as instrumental in shaping her own career trajectory and creative vision.
Beyoncé has also spoken about how Jackson's ability to transcend racial and cultural boundaries inspired her to pursue music on a global scale.
Throughout her career, she has paid homage to his legacy through her own groundbreaking performances and visual presentations, continuing the tradition of artistic excellence that he established.
The feminism and female empowerment themes on Beyoncé's second solo album B'Day were inspired by her role in Dreamgirls and by singer Josephine Baker.
Beyoncé paid homage to Baker by performing "Déjà Vu" at the 2006 Fashion Rocks concert wearing Baker's trademark mini-hula skirt embellished with fake bananas.

The iconic costume choice was both a celebration of Baker's legacy as a groundbreaking Black entertainer and a visual statement about reclaiming and reimagining historical symbols of Black female artistry.
Through this performance, Beyoncé demonstrated how contemporary artists could draw inspiration from their predecessors while creating new meanings and contexts for their work.

Beyoncé has stated that she is personally inspired by US First Lady Michelle Obama, saying "She proves you can do it all" and she has described Oprah Winfrey as "the definition of inspiration and a strong woman".
She has also discussed how Jay Z is a continuing inspiration to her, both with what she describes as his lyrical genius and in the obstacles he has overcome in his life.

Beyoncé has expressed admiration for the artist Jean-Michel Basquiat, posting in a letter "what I find in the work of Jean-Michel Basquiat, I search for in every day in music... he is lyrical and raw".
Beyond these personal influences, Beyoncé has credited her mother, Tina Knowles, as her foundational inspiration, crediting her with instilling confidence and work ethic from an early age.
She has also spoken about how other artists like Prince and David Bowie shaped her artistic vision, particularly in their fearless approach to reinvention and genre-blending.

These varied inspirations—spanning from political figures and entertainment icons to visual artists and family members—have collectively informed the multifaceted creative approach that defines her career across music, film, and visual art.
In 2006, Beyoncé introduced her all-female tour band Suga Mama (also the name of a song in B'Day) which includes bassists, drummers, guitarists, horn players, keyboardists and percussionists.
Her background singers, The Mamas, consist of Montina Cooper-Donnell, Crystal Collins and Tiffany Moniqué Riddick.
The ensemble became known for their high-energy performances and tight musicianship, elevating the production value of Beyoncé's live shows.
This groundbreaking all-female band set a new standard for concert tours and has influenced countless artists to prioritize diverse representation in their backing musicians.
Beyoncé has received praise for her stage presence and voice during live performances.
Jarett Wieselman of the New York Post placed her at number one on her list of the Five Best Singer/Dancers.

According to Barbara Ellen of The Guardian Beyoncé is the most in-charge female artist she's seen onstage, while Alice Jones of The Independent wrote she "takes her role as entertainer so seriously she's almost too good."
Her commitment to perfecting every aspect of her performances has earned her comparisons to legendary entertainers like Michael Jackson and Prince.
Critics consistently note that Beyoncé's meticulous attention to choreography, vocal delivery, and audience engagement sets a standard that few contemporary artists can match.

Described as being "sexy, seductive and provocative" when performing on stage, Beyoncé has said that she originally created the alter ego "Sasha Fierce" to keep that stage persona separate from who she really is.
She described Sasha as being "too aggressive, too strong, too sassy [and] too sexy", stating, "I'm not like her in real life at all."
Sasha was conceived during the making of "Crazy in Love", and Beyoncé introduced her with the release of her 2008 album I Am... Sasha Fierce.
The alter ego allowed Beyoncé to explore more daring artistic directions while maintaining a clear boundary between her public performance identity and her private self.

Though she eventually retired the Sasha Fierce persona in later years, the character became iconic among fans and critics alike, representing a pivotal moment in her artistic evolution and approach to stage presence.
Beyoncé has been described as a having a wide-ranging sex appeal, with music journalist Touré writing that since the release of Dangerously in Love, she has "become a crossover sex symbol".
Offstage Beyoncé says that while she likes to dress sexily, her onstage dress "is absolutely for the stage."
She has emphasized that her provocative performances are an artistic choice rather than a reflection of her personal life, maintaining a distinction between her public persona and private self.
This boundary has allowed her to control her own narrative while continuing to push creative boundaries in her visual presentation and choreography.
